article.title: Elon Musk: Government Checks Are 'Best Way' to Deal With AI Job Losses
article.summary:
Elon Musk thinks he has a solution to AI-driven job losses: the government pays for everyone to live well.
Loading audio narration..."Universal HIGH INCOME via checks issued by the Federal government is the best way to deal with unemployment caused by AI," the Tesla and SpaceX CEO said in a late Thursday post on X.
Universal HIGH INCOME via checks issued by the Federal government is the best way to deal with unemployment caused by AI.
"For those who do lose their jobs, retraining done well preserves agency and self-worth in ways a basic income cannot," he added.
Experts in personal finance and AI told Business Insider that Musk's message was "dangerous and misleading," and emphasized that even if AI delivers abundance, governments will have to effectively distribute the gains.

article.title: IMF, World Bank meetings show limits in mitigating shocks, reliance on US for solutions
article.summary:
What financial assistance are IMF and World Bank providing?
How are geopolitical shocks affecting global economic leadership?
"Actually some of the most important decisions on the global economy are not happening here," Josh Lipsky, international economics chair at the Atlantic Council, said of the IMF and World Bank campus.
"The single most important development in the global economy happened between the U.S. and Iran," he said.
The fund's latest World Economic Outlook said a prolonged war could push the global economy into recession.

article.title: Faisal Islam: What people in power think the impact of the Iran war will be
article.summary:
While there is still much uncertainty from the Gulf, the clouds are less dark now, allowing some to focus on other concerns.
Reeves could even be hopeful after growth figures showed the UK on course for 0.5% to 0.6% growth in the first quarter.
As news emerged yesterday of the reopening of the Strait, energy prices tumbled.
So too did borrowing costs, petrol prices, and mortgage rates.

article.title: The Trump team’s puzzling ‘all is well’ message on the economy
article.summary:
It was a puzzling and dicey political message before the war; it’s even more puzzling and dicey now.
Today, the message is more: Despite what voters are hearing (or seeing at any local gas station), things are actually quite good.
“We had the best economy in the history of our country my first term,” Trump added.
And perhaps more to the point, Americans said 65%-25% that Trump had worsened economic conditions rather than made them better.
The war has clearly made the economy more of a liability for Trump.

article.title: How Will Markets React to Strait of Hormuz Being Closed Again?
article.summary:
After the announcement of the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on Friday, the Dow went up nearly 900 points, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq also rose sharply.
It comes as oil prices also plunged which might mean gas prices will start coming down as well.
NBC’s Allie Canal joins Saturday TODAY to break down what Iran’s new closure of the Strait of Hormuz means for the economy, gas prices and more.

article.title: 6,000 Meters Under the Pacific, Japan Seeks Independence From China on Rare Earths
article.summary:
The feat is set to strengthen Japan's role in the increasingly crucial rare earths sector, a central element in the trade war between China and the United States.
Rare earths are a group of 17 metals critical to advanced technologies.
The embargo caused panic across industries, particularly in the automotive sector, and global prices of rare earths increased tenfold within a year.
The Japanese government has created strategic reserves of rare earths to mitigate any temporary supply disruptions.
Japan is not only an importer of rare earths, but an advanced player in their transformation into high-value-added components.

article.title: One of the most stressful jobs in finance right now: private credit sales
article.summary:
The private credit boom created a lucrative job on Wall Street: selling the asset class to wealthy individuals hungry for returns.
Anxious clientsInvestors have so many questions that they're now reaching out to their old salespeople for advice, according to one former wholesaler at a major private credit firm who now works in private wealth.
This comes after a sustained hiring boom at the intersection of private wealth and credit.
The ex-wholesaler said he has had former colleagues leave private credit due to redemption limits.
"I have heard this from a number of people: I want old souls managing my private credit," Serio said.

article.title: Oil prices jump again and stocks pull back from records as US-Iran peace talks stall
article.summary:
Oil prices jumped and stocks edged back from record levels on Monday after a second round of peace talks between the US and Iran appeared to be on the verge of collapse before they even began.
Crude prices had moderated in recent weeks, falling more than 20% from highs through Friday on renewed optimism for a peace deal.
But the continued closure of the strait has forced several countries to enact energy-saving tactics as prices remain high.
"My Representatives are going to Islamabad, Pakistan — They will be there tomorrow evening, for Negotiations," Trump wrote in a Truth Social Post on Sunday.
However, Iran said hours later that it would not participate in the second round of negotiations with the US.

article.title: Work on Capitol Hill? Your boss may want to ban you from betting on prediction markets
article.summary:
If you work on Capitol Hill, your boss might prohibit you from betting on prediction markets pretty soon.
The White House has denied that any staff are getting rich from prediction market trades, but in March, the administration did warn White House staffers against making prediction market trades.
Democratic Rep. Greg Casar of Texas, the sponsor of a bill that would ban prediction markets that are considered vulnerable to insider trading, indicated he saw little need to lay out an official ban in his office.
Democratic Sen. Jeff Merkley of Oregon, who introduced a bill to ban lawmakers from trading on prediction markets, told Business Insider that he'd prefer to see Congress start there.
"If Congress is willing to go even further and ban top staff from trading, I would support that."

article.title: Inside GitHub's Fake Star Economy
article.summary:
Six million fake stars, $0.06 per click, and a VC funding pipeline that treats GitHub popularity as proof of traction.
Ghost accounts - zero repos, zero followers, no bio - make up about 1% of a healthy project's stargazer base.
When Dagster published its investigation, fake star profiles were deleted within 48 hours - but only after public embarrassment, not proactive detection.
A change that would structurally undermine the fake star economy.
Removing repos but leaving 57% of fake accounts intact preserves the labor force of the fake star economy while doing little to deter repeat offenses.

article.title: Washington steps up scrutiny of prediction markets
article.summary:
A controversy has erupted over prediction markets such as Polymarket and KalshiFILE - An advertisement for prediction market platform Kalshi hangs at 13th and L Streets in northwest Washington, April 1, 2026.

But the scrutiny has drawn focus to the differing approaches of the main prediction markets.
Young acknowledged his proposal is just a first step, and said lawmakers have a lot to learn about prediction markets.

article.title: Zimbabwe facts of the day
article.summary:
Zimbabwe, often considered an economic basket-case because of its history of farm seizures and hyperinflation, is enjoying an idiosyncratic boom.
High prices for the metal and other commodities have led to a surge of cash through its highly informal economy.
They have made it easier for authorities to stop printing money and meddling in currency markets; inflation is at its lowest in about 30 years.
The IMF has repeatedly revised upwards estimates for economic growth, most recently to at least 7.5% for 2025, almost double the African average…Gold is not the only source of growth.
Zimbabwe’s diaspora, mainly in South Africa, sent back $2.5bn last year.

article.title: An expert forecasts how the Iran war could hit your budget
article.summary:
According to March’s Consumer Price Index (CPI), food prices were no higher last month than they had been in February.
The war with Iran has yet to produce any discernible increase in food prices.
So, it takes time for an energy shock to work its way through the supply chain.
So the impact of rising diesel costs may not work its way into that part of the supply chain for weeks.
So, when they pass through the supply chain, consumers can substitute: If beef gets more expensive, they can eat more chicken.

article.title: Gas prices are sky-high. Here are 8 ways to save at the pump.
article.summary:
Right now, he thinks he's at a break-even point; within five or so years, should prices remain elevated, he thinks he'll save thousands annually.
Changing gas prices are reshaping American drivers' everyday lives, whether through cutting back on trips or shaking up their budgets.
Consider what grade of gas your vehicle actually needsBennett suggested considering the recommended versus the actual required octane rating.
Check on your vehicleDrivers saving money on gas "really comes down to keeping their vehicle fuel efficient and running properly," Bennett said.
"If gas prices remain this high or continue to surge, then I will continue to avoid driving whenever possible," he said.

article.title: How Matt Fitzpatrick's PGA Tour win turned up volume in week where money talked
article.summary:
At least the climax to the PGA Tour's RBC Heritage provided a gripping finale to follow the previous week's thrilling Masters.
"I'm paid so much money to be out there in front of those crowds," Fitzpatrick said.
And after the brilliant shootout birdie that beat Scheffler for his most recent win, Fitzpatrick borrowed an analogy from his other sporting passion, football.
PGA Tour purses would not be so grand had Saudi not pumped so much cash into the rival LIV tour.
Oil money has driven rampant inflation that has made millions for so many golfers, especially in the men's game.

article.title: Anthropic’s new cybersecurity model could get it back in the government’s good graces
article.summary:
The Trump administration has spent nearly two months fighting with AI company Anthropic.
But some of the ice may reportedly be melting between the two, thanks to Anthropic’s buzzy new cybersecurity-focused model: Claude Mythos Preview.
Anthropic has recently attempted to get back in the US government’s good graces, at least in some capacity, with Mythos Preview.
The release of Mythos Preview has already reportedly sparked emergency meetings between US bank leaders and Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell.
If Amodei’s meeting opens up conversations about further integrating Anthropic’s Claude into government usage across agencies, it’s possible that the DoD could shift its views on Claude accordingly as well.

article.title: AI Startup Are Moving Into Smaller Neighborhoods to Suit Their Culture
article.summary:
Despite the pandemic, an exodus of tech companies, and a feared "doom loop," a one-two punch of return-to-office mandates and the explosion of AI startups has shifted San Francisco's tech center into smaller neighborhoods.
Companies focused on AI, such as Virtual AI, Applied Compute, Rox AI, and Curai Health, are all located in the same building as Together AI.
Other AI companies with a three-block radius include Scale AI, Resolve AI, and Gamma.
"An office district isn't an office district anymore — it's a mixed-use area," said Florida.
Between the Mission District and the Castro, several up-and-coming AI companies have also quietly set up offices along the main street, including Comp AI and Ace AI, just a few blocks away from the AI Start Academy.

article.title: I've lived in China for over 10 years and saw the OpenClaw craze unfold. The West needs to keep up.
article.summary:
This as-told-to essay is based on a conversation with Thomas Derksen, a German entrepreneur who has lived in China for over a decade.
There's so much happening in China, and I'm always interested in how things are different in the West and in Asia.
People in China are more curious, and they have a strong business mindset.
Some people don't think normal jobs will give them a good future, so they try OpenClaw to build something.
In China, a crayfish shop owner sees a tool and thinks: Maybe I can use this to grow my business.

article.title: This charming gadget writes bad AI poetry
article.summary:
I’ve never been as charmed and frustrated by one gadget as I have with the Poetry Camera.
I tried to set this aside and accept the Poetry Camera free of bias, but maybe I was just never going to have a good time with it.
The Poetry Camera puts together words that kind of sound deep and meaningful on the surface, but also feel soulless and read like empty calories.
AI can be a powerful tool in making software, but writing meaningful poetry requires, at a bare minimum, a soul.
I’m still not sure what Poetry Camera is, but I do know one thing: it’s not for me.

article.title: How Big Wall Street Banks, From JPMorgan to Citi, Are Using AI
article.summary:
Wall Street is betting big on, and pouring billions into, AI — and leaders have had to answer for their spending.
During banks' first-quarter earnings last week, analysts pressed some executives on how those giant investments are paying off, whether in productivity gains or head count changes.
April's earnings calls came just one week after many Wall Street CEOs attended an impromptu meeting at the Treasury to discuss the powerful new AI model.
As banks rapidly weave generative AI into everything from trading floors to back offices, the technology is reshaping not just workflows, but workplace culture.
Here's what we know about how Wall Street banks are embracing AI.

article.title: Deezer says AI song uploads have nearly overtaken human music
article.summary:
Deezer says it receives nearly 75,000 AI-generated song submissions to its music streaming platform each day, accounting for about 44 percent of all daily uploads, as reported earlier by TechCrunch.
Despite the increase in “fraudulent” uploads, Deezer says the consumption of AI songs makes up around 1 to 3 percent of total streams, as the platform continues to remove AI-generated music from its recommendation algorithm.
Deezer positions the tool as setting an “industry standard,” adding that the platform is currently the “only” music streaming service tagging AI-generated tracks.
The service also demonetizes AI-generated songs and has stopped storing high-resolution versions of them.
Since launching its tool in January 2025, Deezer says the number of AI-generated tracks uploaded daily has jumped from 10,000 to 75,000.

article.title: Fortnite developers can make AI characters now — just don’t try to date them
article.summary:
You must not create a persona designed to provide medical or mental health guidance.
You must not create a persona that role-plays as, simulates, or impersonates a date, romantic partner, or other intimate companion.
You must not attempt to circumvent or undermine our safety systems, including intentionally designing your persona to bypass our content restrictions.

article.title: Baumgartner Introduces Bipartisan Bill to Tighten Controls on Sensitive Chipmaking Equipment
article.summary:
Baumgartner Introduces Bipartisan Bill to Tighten Controls on Sensitive Chipmaking Equipment | Press ReleasesWASHINGTON – Congressman Michael Baumgartner (WA‑05) introduced the Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware (MATCH) Act, a bipartisan bill designed to strengthen U.S. national security by closing critical gaps in export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment (SME).
For too long, our export controls have been a patchwork of entity-based restrictions that Beijing easily bypasses using front companies.
When foreign suppliers of advanced chipmaking tools are not subject to the same rules as U.S. firms, it undermines the objectives of U.S. export controls.
Allied export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment suffer from meaningful gaps that China continues to exploit.
We must ensure our allies align their export controls with American standards — through diplomacy, if possible, but unilaterally if necessary.

article.title: Schneier on Security
article.summary:
This is, in many respects, exactly the kind of responsible disclosure that security researchers have long urged.
Anthropic said security contractors agreed with the AI’s severity rating 198 times, with an 89 per cent severity agreement.
The security problem is far greater than one company and one model.
The security company Aisle was able to replicate many of Anthropic’s published anecdotes using smaller, cheaper, public AI models.
Nor should such a company be able to restrict the ability of society to make choices about its own security.

article.title: What is Mythos and why are experts worried about Anthropic’s AI model
article.summary:
Instead of a public rollout, Anthropic is using its Project Glasswing initiative to offer a small group of organizations access to its Mythos AI model for cybersecurity testing.
Not since OpenAI temporarily withheld its GPT-2 model in 2019 has a major developer deemed a system too dangerous for the public.
It also scored 31 percentage points higher than Anthropic’s previous cutting-edge model, Opus 4.6, on the USAMO 2026 Mathematical Olympiad, a grueling, two-day proof-based competition.
Prior to April 2025, no AI model could complete those tasks at all.
Neither expert denies that Mythos is a significant advance, but suggest the decisive regulatory action is partly driven by institutional self-preservation.

article.title: AI hit software engineers first. Here's what they want you to know.
article.summary:
Software engineers have a message for white-collar workers: Get ready.
Since late last year, advances in tools like Claude Code and Codex have transformed how engineers do their jobs.
He said that he could see AI tools like OpenClaw replacing executive assistants by handling scheduling and coordinating with stakeholders.
Data from tech hiring analytics firm TrueUp shows software engineering job postings are at their highest level in more than three years, with over 67,000 open roles.
"There's practically infinite demand," Bendov said, adding that AI has opened more "opportunity for people who aren't professional engineers."

article.title: Dairy Queen is putting an AI chatbot in its drive-thrus
article.summary:
Dairy Queen is becoming the latest fast food chain to get in on AI, as it’s bringing a chatbot to dozens of its drive-thrus across the US and Canada.
It aims to help speed up drive-thru service and “encourage customers to add more food to their orders,” according to The Wall Street Journal.
Following a test last year, Dairy Queen is widely launching the tech built by Presto, an AI company that already works with chains like Carl’s Jr., Hardee’s, Taco John’s, and Fazoli’s.
In 2023, a report from Bloomberg revealed that Presto’s AI drive-thrus may actually be assisted by human workers based in locations like the Philippines.
Presto’s chatbot takes orders correctly about 90 percent of the time, The Wall Street Journal reports.

A line of robotaxis and aspiring self-driving cars were parked outside Ride AI.

article.title: Broadstaff CEO Says Laid-Off Tech Workers Can Pivot to Data Centers
article.summary:
A talent CEO says all the recently laid-off tech workers wading through corporate America's sluggish job market should look to one growing area: data centers.
Data center job listings increased by 64% between 2023 and 2025, Deloitte found.
Employment at data centers in the US increased by over 60% from 2016 to 2023, according to the US Census Bureau.
Data center technician jobs are growingA data center technician does on-site tech support and hardware maintenance.
For instance, Broadstaff works with Wachter, a national electrical contractor that works with large data centers and offers apprenticeship programs.

article.title: The strange way cocaine water pollution is changing salmon
article.summary:
It turns out that salmon exposed to cocaine through water pollution do a lot of swimming—which may not be a good thingStrange but true: salmon on cocaine swim a lot more.
This sounds like an odd thing to research, but cocaine and its metabolites—along with other drugs and pharmaceuticals—are widespread in the environment, mainly from human wastewater.
In the new study, Brand and his colleagues focused on wild Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar).
In one group of 35, the implant slowly released cocaine, in another it released benzoylecgonine (cocaine’s main metabolite), and in a control group it released nothing.
And recent research demonstrated how cocaine and its metabolites can accumulate in wild fish such as sharks.

article.title: 6 Facts About Climate Change
article.summary:
Read on to learn more about the history and consequences of climate change, as well as how it might be averted.
Research has shown that by the late 1970s, the oil company Exxon was aware that its fossil fuels could cause climate change that could have “dramatic environmental effects.” In 1988, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) was formed to address the Earth's rising temperatures.
Most people believe climate change is realThere is indeed a small, but noisy, group of people who believe climate change is not real.
However, in reality, the majority of people believe in climate change and would like to see actions taken to fix it.
“To avoid the worst consequences of climate change, we’ll need to reach ‘net zero’ carbon emissions by 2050 or sooner.

article.summary:
It was a proposal from Meta to build one of the largest-ever AI data centers in the world.
Loading...Maine's Democratic-controlled Legislature approved a pause on most data center construction — the strongest action taken against data centers at the state level.
Loading...Virginia is ground zero for data centers, but opposition is mountingVirginia, the state with the most data centers by far, is considering walking back some measures that made the state so attractive for investment.

article.title: OpenAI’s former Sora boss is leaving
article.summary:
I am immensely grateful to Sam, Mark, Aditya and Jakub for fostering a research environment that allowed us to pursue ideas off-the-beaten path from the company’s mainline roadmap.
It’s tempting in life to mode collapse to the most important thing, but cultivating entropy is the only way for a research lab to thrive long-term, and Sam deeply understands this.
Sora was a project that could not have happened anywhere but OpenAI, and I will always deeply love this place for that.

article.title: Now Brief for your Samsung TV is a reality with this SmartThings update
article.summary:
Soon, users with a Samsung TV from 2024 or later will find Now Brief on their displays with a recap of the night.
Samsung is rolling out its newest SmartThings update, and the company is highlighting a feature that's found a new home: Now Brief.
The Galaxy S26 series is seemingly spearheading this feature's debut, as Samsung says Now Brief will support "Home Security, Family Care, and Pet Care."
Android Central's Take This Family Care update was pretty interesting.
In particular, it says Care Insights delivers more actionable and long-term data within the Family Care environment.

article.title: Why do gray whales keep dying in San Francisco's waters?
article.summary:
While a lack of food may be driving whales into the bay, it's not necessarily starvation that's killing them.
In recent years, nearly one-fifth of the gray whales that have swum into the San Francisco Bay have died there, usually after being struck by ships, according to Slaathaug's study published in the journal Frontiers in Marine Science this week.

article.title: Common cleaning sponge found to release trillions of microplastic fibers
article.summary:
As these sponges wear down, they can release tiny plastic particles into the environment.
Researchers estimate that more than a trillion microplastic fibers may be released worldwide every month as people use and discard these everyday cleaning tools.
These fragments can break down further into microplastic fibers, which are tiny strands of plastic often too small to see.
Denser sponges held up better over time and released fewer microplastic fibers, while less dense versions broke down more quickly.
Using Amazon sales from August 2023 as a reference point, they calculated that approximately 1.55 trillion microplastic fibers could be released each month.

article.title: A Humanoid Robot Set a Half-Marathon Record in China
article.summary:
Over the weekend in China, a humanoid robot shattered world half-marathon record—the human record—by seven minutes.
The event consisted of more than 100 humanoid robots from 76 institutions across China.
Run, Robot, RunA humanoid robot is designed to mimic the structure and movement of the human body, with legs, arms, and sensors that allow it to interact with its environment.
The winning robot, "Blitz,” from smartphone manufacturer Honor was on display at the awards ceremony after the Beijing E-Town Robot Half Marathon.
But a humanoid robot is designed to mimic humans.

article.title: Leicester team finds ancient freshened water under ocean floor
article.summary: The water is thought to be tens of thousands of years old, and while the process of how it got there is not yet fully understood, it is hoped analysis of the samples will help scientists learn more about climate change.

article.title: Nothing’s modular CMF Headphone Pro are down to their lowest price to date
article.summary:
Over the past several years, Nothing’s budget-centric subsidiary, CMF, has built a reputation for producing attractive, inexpensive products with features often reserved for gadgets that cost twice the price.
Although the Headphone Pro eschew the retro-transparent design found on Nothing wares like the Headphone 1, I wouldn’t necessarily say that’s a bad thing given the polarizing, squared-off design of the latter.
Instead, the Pro go all in on the curves, with circular earcups, cushions, and a plush headband.
The biggest draw of the Headphone Pro, aside from their adaptive ANC, is that they offer a whopping 100 hours of battery life per charge, ensuring you can travel just about anywhere without having to top them off.
The fact that they support Google Fast Pair and the higher-quality LDAC codec is just a plus.

article.title: Mandelson Failed Security Vetting: The Prime Minister Speaks Out
article.summary:
Available for over a yearToday, Keir Starmer says it's "staggering" he was not told Lord Mandelson had failed security vetting for his appointment as US ambassador.
Speaking from a summit in France, the prime minister says he's "absolutely furious" and will update MPs in Parliament on Monday.
Opposition leaders are calling on Starmer to resign, accusing him of "catastrophically poor judgement" and of being "incompetent, gullible or a liar.

article.title: A mine despoiled the beauty of the rainforest. This Goldman Prize winner took action
article.summary:
This Goldman Prize winner took actiontoggle caption Goldman Environmental PrizeTheonila Roka Matbob was born in what should have been a lush rainforest.
"You have to go miles — into another region and territory — to find the trees, the forest," says Roka Matbob, who is now 35.
Her work as an activist to repair the environmental and social harms has earned her The Goldman Environmental Prize for 2026.
"Theonila is leading a historic effort to obtain justice for decades of environmental and social devastation because of the Panguna mine," says Ilan Kayatsky, of the Goldman Environmental Prize, in a statement to NPR.
toggle caption Goldman Environmental PrizeThis Goldman honor comes with a cash prize.

article.title: Sharks and tuna are overheating and running out of options
article.summary:
As ocean temperatures climb, these species are more likely to overheat, which could shrink their habitable range and push them toward cooler regions closer to the poles.
What Makes "Mesothermic" Fish UniqueThe study, published in the journal Science, focuses on "mesothermic" fishes.
These are a rare group, making up less than 0.1% of all fish species, that can retain body heat and keep parts of themselves warmer than the surrounding water.
These thresholds represent the water temperatures at which large fish can no longer release heat quickly enough to maintain stable body temperatures.
Looking ahead, the researchers expect that suitable habitats for large mesothermic fish will shrink as global temperatures continue to rise, especially during warmer months.

article.title: Acting ICE director says he's resigning amid record-breaking shutdown
article.summary:
Acting ICE director says he's resigning amid record-breaking shutdowntoggle caption Tom Brenner/APPresident Trump's top Immigration and Customs Enforcement official, Todd Lyons, said he would resign on Thursday as acting director, adding to the list of leadership shakeups at the Department of Homeland Security.
Under his tenure, the agency took the lead in Trump's mass deportation agenda, rapidly scaling up sweeping arrests across the country.
Madison Sheahan, former deputy director at Immigration and Customs Enforcement, left her post at the start of the year to run for Congress.
The ICE director must be confirmed by the Senate; Lyons had only been serving in an acting capacity.
There has not been a Senate-confirmed ICE director since the Obama administration.

article.title: Facebook to remove imposter accounts tied to immigration scams, DA's office says
article.summary:
Meta, the parent company of Facebook and WhatsApp, has pledged to remove imposter accounts tied to scams that were recently flagged by the Manhattan district attorney's office, following a phone call between the two parties this week, the DA's office told ABC News Friday.
Manhattan District Attorney Alvin Bragg last week accused Meta of failing to remove imposter accounts that Bragg said pose as organizations like Catholic Charities to offer fake immigration services that scam money from unsuspecting victims.
"These imposter accounts have led to tens of thousands of dollars of fraudulent transfers," Bragg said in a letter to Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg demanding the company take action after requests to remove false profiles were declined.
In some instances, scammers create public Facebook accounts that mirror real accounts belonging to pro bono legal services organizations, Bragg said.
Bragg is the latest prosecutor to go public with criticism of Meta for failing to protect the public from criminals lurking on its social media platforms.

article.title: How Americans really feel about immigration
article.summary:
Immigration enforcement was once one of President Donald Trump’s strongest issues, driving his victories in the 2016 and 2024 presidential contests.
But these days, most Americans seem to hate just about everything Trump’s administration has done to actually address the issue.
Polls show Americans have shifted dramatically on immigration since Trump returned to office — and about half of all Americans now want to abolish ICE, the deportation force Trump has empowered since returning to office.
But what do Americans really think about immigration, beyond their feelings about the current administration?
Not only can this constituency not vote, but Americans generally tend to really underestimate, I think, how interconnected we all are with the immigration system.

article.summary:
Tufts University doctoral student Rumeysa Ozturk reads from a prepared statement following a court hearing outside the John Joseph Moakley United States Courthouse, Dec. 4, 2025, in Boston.

Rumeysa Ozturk, the Tufts University student and Palestinian rights advocate who was arrested by masked federal agents in March 2025 has voluntarily returned to her native Turkey after completing her Ph.D. and reaching a settlement agreement with the Trump administration, the ACLU announced Friday.

article.title: Israel starts a tense ceasefire in Lebanon. And, Trump nominates a new CDC director.
article.summary:
Israel starts a tense ceasefire in Lebanon.
Today's top storiesA 10-day ceasefire to pause fighting between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon begins today.
President Trump announced the deal yesterday on social media after he had separate phone calls with leaders of Israel and Lebanon.
Iran has said it would not negotiate peace with the U.S. unless Israel entered a ceasefire in Lebanon.
❓ Quiz: This newsletter has kept you updated on the latest Iran war news.

article.title: The foreign students powering American summer jobs may be harder to find this year
article.summary:
Small and seasonal US businesses rely on about 100,000 international students to boost their staff each summer.
The student workers cover a wide range of hospitality and tourism roles, including lifeguards, bartenders, theme park attendants, retail staffers, and restaurant servers.
Although J-1 is a non-immigrant visa, Simon said delays in the vetting process could disrupt the pipeline.
Hold-ups at a US embassy could mean student workers arrive later than scheduled, forcing businesses to operate at limited capacity.
Additionally, Simon said many of the student workers depend on the money they earn during the summer.

article.title: Trump Admin Actively Re-Vetting Biden Migrants Given Green Cards
article.summary:
President Donald Trump’s United States Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) is actively re-vetting migrants awarded green cards, asylum, and other benefits under former President Joe Biden, the agency’s director confirmed.
Over the weekend, USCIS Director Joe Edlow told One America News that the Trump administration has kicked its fraud probes into high gear, specifically going after fraudsters who came to the U.S. under the Biden administration.
“And even if you’ve already [committed fraud], and you think you’ve gotten away with it, we’re going back,” Edlow said.
“As you noted earlier, we are looking at old cases, we are going back and re-vetting cases for people who were granted green cards and granted other benefits during the Biden administration, when there was no vetting.
“Our Fraud Detection and National Security team completed investigations into more than 21,000 cases, identifying fraud in 65% of them,” he revealed.

article.title: Why the New Criminal Charges Against an ICE Agent in Minneapolis Could Be a True Turning Point
article.summary:
On Thursday, Minnesota state prosecutors announced a historic set of charges against a federal immigration agent.
As he drove in an unmarked SUV, Morgan encountered rush-hour traffic, which he decided to bypass by unlawfully driving on the highway’s shoulder.
And under the Constitution’s supremacy clause, federal law enforcement are in fact protected from prosecution so long as their actions are authorized by federal law and necessary and proper to fulfill their official duties.
“It’s hard to argue that brandishing a firearm during a traffic misunderstanding is either allowed by federal law or necessary and proper for fulfilling ICE’s duties,” he said.
If the Cadillac passengers had known they were dealing with a federal agent, the entire incident with Morgan could have very well been avoided.

article.title: Shipping Antimatter by Truck to Understand the Universe
article.summary:
A truck makes a historic trip around CERN's facility on the France-Switzerland border, transporting the world's most expensive material for the first time.
The antimatter inside is made by CERN's enormous particle accelerator, and then antimatter particles are decelerated and captured for storage, shipment and study.
Annihilation is routine at CERN's antimatter factory, happening on a small scale with individual particles and showing up as a line on a graph (pictured below).
So far, the science shows that matter and its antimatter equivalents are identical opposites in weight and magnetism.
The search for these discrepancies means that the antimatter particles must leave their birthplace at CERN because the same enormous magnets necessary to produce antimatter also make it difficult to study due to magnetic interference.

article.title: Record number of Rohingya refugees died at sea last year, UNHCR says
article.summary:
The UN refugee agency has warned of an ‘unmarked graveyard for thousands of desperate Rohingya refugees’ in South AsiaThe United Nations refugee agency has revealed that nearly 900 Rohingya refugees were reported dead or missing in the Bay of Bengal and the Andaman Sea in 2025.
Speaking to reporters in Geneva, the UNHCR’s spokesperson, Babar Baloch, described the area as an “unmarked graveyard for thousands of desperate Rohingya refugees”, noting that some 5,000 are thought to have drowned at sea over the last decade.
Hundreds of thousands of Rohingya refugees began fleeing Myanmar in 2017 amid an ethnic cleansing campaign.
They largely settled in refugee camps in Bangladesh, which continues to give refuge to those fleeing today.
AdvertisementDeadly journeysEarlier this month, an overcrowded trawler carrying about 250 Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals sank in the Andaman Sea.

article.title: Trump administration ends Miami Catholic Charities funding. Here's why
article.summary:
ETThe Trump administration didn't renew a contract with the Catholic Charities in Miami.
The Trump administration canceled millions of dollars in funding to Catholic Charities in Miami.
The Catholic Charities received $11 million from HHS in fiscal year 2025, according to federal spending data, but the award ended as of March 31.
What have Catholic Charities in Miami historically done?
Wenski wrote that the stripping of funding will force the Archdiocese of Miami’s Catholic Charities’ services for unaccompanied minors to shut down in three months.

article.title: Judge says White House can’t ‘strong-arm’ Apple into blocking ICE trackers
article.summary:
A judge has ruled that the Trump administration should not have coerced Apple and Google into taking down apps which track the activity of ICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement).
In a classic demonstration of the Streisand Effect, the app shot to the top of the App Store when it was condemned by the White House.
The House Judiciary Committee then launched an investigation into whether the DOJ applied unlawful pressure to Apple and Google to remove these apps.
Judge grants developers an injunctionThe creators of ICE Sightings and Eyes Up took the matter to court, arguing that the government was suppressing speech protected by the First Amendment.
A judge has granted the makers of the “ICE Sightings – Chicagoland” Facebook group and the Eyes Up app a preliminary injunction to stop the Trump administration from coercing platforms to take these projects down.

article.title: Born in south Lebanon, displaced to Beirut, two grandmothers reflect on Israeli invasions
article.summary:
Born in south Lebanon, displaced to Beirut, two grandmothers reflect on Israeli invasionstoggle caption Claire Harbage/NPRBEIRUT, Lebanon — Mariam Allawiya and Kafa Wehbe sit on a sun-drenched balcony, smoking.
But this is not how they expected to grow old: Squatting in a vacant building in central Beirut, displaced many times.
Sponsor MessageNow, with a fresh ceasefire, both Israel and Hezbollah are warning displaced people not to return south.
Born in the south, displaced to Beirut, now fleeing againAllawiya was born in the Lebanese village of Maroun al-Ras, near the Israeli border.
Israeli troops invaded in 1982, destroyed her family's house, and occupied south Lebanon for 18 years after that.

article.title: Fifteen South American people deported from the US arrive in DR Congo
article.summary:
An official at the DRC migration agency confirmed the arrivals but did not provide details, The Associated Press news agency reported.
The DRC Ministry of Communications announced earlier this month that it would temporarily accept migrants deported from the US.
The news agency Reuters has reported that the DRC was set to receive more than 30 deportees this week.
The International Organization for Migration (IOM) said that the DRC asked the UN agency for humanitarian assistance with the migrants.
Deportation dealsOther countries in Africa, including Ghana, Rwanda, South Sudan and Uganda, have also accepted migrants deported from the US.

In the U.S., this code is usually assigned to travelers visiting countries that pose security risks to the U.S.

“It was still one of the strangest interactions I’ve ever had at an airport,” Limitless noted.
For example, the first commentator stated the following:“This is a major problem for an intelligence officer who needs to recruit an asset.

article.title: Senior official ousted over Peter Mandelson security row to face MPs
article.summary: An outright failure of the vetting process "would have to be conveyed to the political level", Lord McDonald added - and the fact it appears not to have been indicates "the picture was more complicated than Number 10 wished to present".

article.title: Mandelson saga is a messy palaver - and the questions continue to swirl
article.summary: And all this was going on at just the point that Donald Trump was about to begin his second term in the White House – the very reason Lord Mandelson was given the job in the first place, as he was seen as the political savvy, charming, wily consigliere who could win over the White House.

article.title: Keir Starmer would have blocked Peter Mandelson over vetting , says David Lammy
article.summary: Speculating about why Foreign Office officials awarded the clearance, she said it was possible there was a view the risks associated with Mandelson were "priced in", and it was for them to do "what the prime minister wanted", before putting in place "any mitigations in place to make sure there weren't any security concerns".

article.title: UAE officials reportedly warned they may be forced to use yuan or other currencies if they run low on dollars amid the Iran war
article.summary:
The United Arab Emirates apparently dropped a hint the dollar’s dominance isn’t assured in the global oil trade if fallout from the Iran war gets worse.
To be sure, the UAE has plenty of money, including $270 billion in foreign-exchange reserves and trillions of dollars across its sovereign wealth funds.
UAE officials also pointed out the U.S. started the Iran war and said they may be forced to use China’s yuan or other currencies for oil transactions if the availability of dollars gets tight, sources told the Journal.
Saudi Arabia’s decision in 1974 to price its exports in dollars helped establish the dollar as the standard across the global oil trade.
But the Iran war could worsen some cracks that had already been forming in the so-called petrodollar regime, analysts at Deutsche Bank warned last month.

article.title: What to know as war with Iran stretches into week 8
article.summary:
What to know as war with Iran stretches into week 8President Donald Trump speaks to the media, before boarding Air Force One on his way to Virginia, at Joint Base Andrews in Maryland, April 10, 2026.
The United States, Israel and Iran are now in their eighth week of conflict.
Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff General Dan Caine displays a map showing the blockade line in the Strait of Hormuz during a briefing on the Iran war, at the Pentagon in Washington, April 16, 2026.
The president has said publicly that Iran will receive no payments in any form, but privately, officials say the issue is being discussed.
They said, however, Iran would not reap any financial benefits without first demonstrating compliance with other terms of the deal.

article.title: Iran says it has closed the Strait of Hormuz again, as ceasefire nears its end
article.summary:
Speaking to reporters in the Oval Office Saturday, Trump said Iran had tried to get "cute" in taking back control of the Strait of Hormuz.
Despite hinting at the possibility of resuming attacks on Iran, Trump also told reporters "I think it's going to happen," referring to a deal.
Hezbollah, meanwhile, urged people displaced by the fighting not to rush back to southern Lebanon, the Bekaa Valley and Beirut's southern suburbs until the situation becomes clearer.
The latest round of fighting began after Hezbollah fired rockets into northern Israel following the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran on Feb. 28.
Israeli forces responded with airstrikes and a ground invasion of southern Lebanon.

article.title: Pakistan PM, army chief wrap up key trips in push for more US-Iran talks
article.summary:
Field Marshal Asim Munir leaves Tehran while premier Shehbaz Sharif heads home from Turkiye amid hopes of another round of US-Iran talks.
Munir held talks with the country’s president, foreign minister, parliament speaker and head of Iran’s military central command centre.
But diplomacy has continued, with Pakistan’s Prime Minister, Shehbaz Sharif, visiting Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkiye to push the peace process.
Donald Trump has said a second round of talks with Iran could be held in Pakistan in the coming days.
Reporting from Islamabad, Al Jazeera’s Kamal Hyder said Munir landed back home on Saturday as Pakistan prepared for another round of US-Iran talks expected “within the next few days”.

article.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 50 of the US-Iran conflict?
article.summary:
The Strait was reopened earlier on Saturday subject to conditions, in line with the Lebanon ceasefire and previously coordinated maritime routes.
In the GulfAdvertisementFragile recovery: Saudi Finance Minister Mohammed Al-Jadaan welcomes Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz, but says the situation remains fragile.
In IsraelIsrael ‘prohibited’ from striking Lebanon: Trump says Israel is barred by the US from continuing attacks on Lebanon, declaring “enough is enough”.
Lebanon war ‘last anti-colonial battle’: Analyst Rami Khouri says the conflict reflects resistance by Iran-aligned groups against decades of Western influence, with southern Lebanon at its core.
Boeing’s war boost: The war has so far boosted Boeing’s defence business and hasn’t affected deliveries to airline customers facing high jet fuel prices, the company’s CEO said.

article.title: Congress extends controversial surveillance powers for 10 days
article.summary:
Congress extends controversial surveillance powers for 10 daystoggle caption J. Scott Applewhite/APCongress has voted to extend a controversial surveillance program until April 30.
That left a stop-gap measure for Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), which was set to expire Monday.
The tool allows U.S. intelligence agencies to intercept the electronic communications of foreign nationals located outside of the United States.
Sponsor MessageLike past reauthorizations, FISA 702's renewal has sparked a protracted debate on Capitol Hill over if and how the tool should be modified.
The intelligence community has argued that would inhibit the efficacy of the tool and endanger national security.

article.title: 'Completely open' didn't last very long in the Strait of Hormuz. Here's the situation.
article.summary:
Iran announced that the Strait of Hormuz was "completely open" on Friday morning, but by Monday, maritime traffic in the strategic waterway ground largely to a halt.
MarineTraffic data from Friday afternoon showed that just over half a dozen commercial vessels had transited the strait since the announcement.
Data from KPler showed that eight commercial vessels had crossed as of 2 pm EST Friday compared to five on Thursday.
But by the evening, new data showed ships were turning back.
On Saturday, Iran announced that it had returned the strait to its "previous state" under the control of its armed forces.

article.title: World reacts to Strait of Hormuz reopening amid US-Iran conflict
article.summary:
Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and United States President Donald Trump have said that the Strait of Hormuz is open to commercial vessels.
Trump affirmed on social media that the strait was open, later claiming that Iran had agreed to “never close the Strait of Hormuz again”.
He said he wanted US involvement in the international mission to secure shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
FinlandFinnish President Alexander Stubb, who participated in the Paris summit, said on X, “We welcome Iran’s announcement on opening the Strait.
Since the outbreak of the conflict, we have followed the guidance of our security partners in the region, and the recommendation so far has been to avoid transiting the Strait of Hormuz.
